title: Classic Hummus

subs.name: 400 g white kidney beans (lobia) for a milder dip
2 tbsp roasted peanut butter or sesame seeds blended with oil
subs.notes: tahini is available at supermarkets and Middle Eastern grocers in India

subs.name: 2 tbsp white vinegar
subs.notes: use half the quantity and adjust to taste

ingredients.name: red chilli powder or paprika, for garnish

steps.text: If using dried chickpeas, soak overnight and pressure-cook for 4–5 whistles until very soft; drain and reserve the liquid. Canned chickpeas can be drained but save a few tablespoons of the liquid.
Place the chickpeas, tahini, lemon juice, garlic and cumin in a blender or food processor. Add a pinch of salt and 3 tablespoons of the reserved liquid.
Blend for 3–4 minutes, scraping down the sides once, until the hummus is completely smooth and pale — longer blending gives a creamier result. Taste and adjust salt and lemon.
Spread into a shallow bowl with the back of a spoon, making a swirl. Drizzle olive oil over the top, dust with chilli powder or paprika, and serve with pita, bread or vegetables.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: tahini → 2 tbsp roasted peanut butter or sesame seeds blended with oil (tahini is available at supermarkets and Middle Eastern grocers in India); chickpea → 400 g white kidney beans (lobia) for a milder dip (same process); lemon → 2 tbsp white vinegar (use half the quantity and adjust to taste)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Chickpeas (kabuli chana) are easy to find in every Indian grocery. Use a domestic mixie for blending — blend in short bursts to prevent motor overheating. Sesame paste (til paste) is a workable substitute for tahini if needed.
